speaker: interviewer
text: It's December the 12th, 1995. To survivor is Shlomo Berger. Interviewer, Masha Loen. In Los Angeles, California USA. It will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that on? This is Shlomo Berger. It's December 12, 1995. I'm interviewing him-- I'm Masha Loen, for the Shoah Foundation. And it's in Los Angeles, California.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. Tell me when you want to start.

speaker: interviewee
text: I look at her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Berger, I want you to tell me, from the beginning, as much as you can remember-- your childhood, and so on. And then we'll go even farther. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my name is Shlomo Berger. I was born on October the 28, 1919, in Krosno-- which was a small town, about 200 kilometers east of Krakow. I was one of nine children-- five sisters and four brothers. And two sisters left for America in 1921. I was two years old. One sister left for Germany. And then another sister left for Germany. They lived in Berlin. And one sister lived in Krosno. The first time I remember my life, going back, was when I was three years old. My father had a tailor shop. And he had many people working. And I said, well--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father? My father's name was Jacob. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you stop for a minute? I'll disconnect.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. Jacob, you started with your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Jacob. My mother's name was Rosa. Now, my father had two wives. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The first--the first wife that he had, her name was Miriam. They were married, I don't know, for how many years. And they had five girls. The girls' names was-- the oldest one was Helena, the second one was Frances, the third one was Eleanor, the fourth one was Berta, the next one was Rose. Unfortunately, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my father's wife, the first one, she died early. And he was left with five girls. So the family convinced my mother, which was 17 years old, to marry my father, and take care of the five girls. After they got married, which is in the 1900s-- approximately, I think, 1910. They had four boys. My oldest brother's name was Moses. He was born in Krosno, 1912. My second brother was Joshua-- he was born in 1915 in Budapest at the time of the First World War, when they evacuated to Hungary. Because Krosno was under the Hungarian kingdom or whatever the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Franz Josef.

speaker: interviewee
text: Franz Josef, Franz Josef I, of Austria. And the district that we lived was Galicia. And they came back. My father was in the First World War. He was stationed in [NON-ENGLISH]-- that is near Vienna. He came home in 1918, after the war ended. And I was born in 1919, in Krosno, on October the 28th. After that, two years later, my youngest brother, Michael, was born. And so we were four brothers living at home, and one sister-- sister-- sister, Berta.

speaker: interviewer
text: You started telling us an experience, when you were three years old. Remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now, there was a practice in-- actually, my parents were orthodox. And there was a practice in Poland that at three years old, you're supposed to start cheder. And I did not want to go to cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father asked one of the employees to take me to cheder. And I remember, he put me under his arm, and he carried me all the way to cheder. And I was crying all the way to cheder. I didn't want to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: But after I already went into cheder, I liked it. I attended every day. Cheder started at 8 o'clock in the morning, till about 3, 4 o'clock in the afternoon. I was very studious. And at five and a half, I remember I started to Chumash. And there was a big party. They used to-- they used to call it an [NON-ENGLISH]. And I started Chumash.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain what Chumash is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Chumash is the study of the five books of Moses. And every Jewish boy at that time in Galicia, at five or five and a half, if he was capable enough, he started to learn the five books of Moses. The next thing in my memory was when I was seven years old. I was seven years old. And I started school. Actually, in Poland, you had to be seven years old to start school. And school started in September. I was born in October. So I actually was not seven. I was not quite seven. But my mother convinced the school principal to accept me. So I started school before seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was public school. I finished seventh school, elementary school. That was the-- that was the compulsory education in Poland. After that, if anybody wanted additional education, you had to go to the gymnasium. Gymnasium was not, anymore, public. It was public, but it was private. You had to pay. And my father was not rich enough, probably, to pay. And on top of it, maybe he would allow me to go to gymnasium. But there was a hangup. You have to go to school six days a week, including Saturday. And being an Orthodox Jew, I could not go to school on Saturday. So I did not go into the gymnasium. The same story was in public school. Public school was six days a week, including Saturday. And what the teacher did, every main subject-- math or some important subject-- he scheduled it for Saturday, so Jewish kids should not know the subjects and should fail.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it enabled for us to be able to make the homework, and know what to do, we had to get in touch with the-- with the Christian boys, and find out what kind of studies-- home studies-- were provided to learn at home. And in this way, we had to learn on our own. Because on Monday, we had to know the subjects that were taught on Saturday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in elementary school, you could still manage to do that. But in high school, it was impossible. Me, wanted an education, not going to the gymnasium, and I was good in math-- I went to a private accounting school for two or two and a half years. I finished accounting with top grades, first one in my class.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's in Krosno?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krosno. And after I finished accounting, I tried to get a job. Impossible. The city had a population of approximately 12,000 people. Out of this were approximately 3,000 to 4,000 Jews. The Jews lived all in the center of town, and all the way around were Poles, Christians. All the small businesses, all the-- all kind of trades in town were all Jewish. All the way around, the Poles were-- had all different type of farmers and educators, and a lot of professions. Jews, primarily, in Krosno, were involved in-- in grocery stores, tailor shops, barbers, shoemakers, and all kinds of other trades. Well, I tried to get a job. But in Poland, at that time, nobody needed to keep any books for tax purposes. The only people had to keep books were big companies, or banks. They had a few Polish banks in town that would not hire any Jews. They had one Jewish bank, that the only time you could get a job in a Jewish bank, as a bookkeeper or as an accountant, if their father was rich, he was a director of the bank. Or you could get a job in a big factory only if their father was a director of the factory. Unfortunately, my father was not a director and he wasn't rich. So I couldn't get a job doing what I really wanted to do. Eventually, I am 16 years old-- well, 16 and 1/2 years old-- and you've got to start making a living. You have to start learning something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father said, well, Shlomo, there's no use playing around. You've got to learn something. All the other-- all you other-- my younger brother was still in school. My older brothers were already working in my father's shop.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father said to me, well, you got to learn a trade. It will save you from two things in your life-- you will never get rich, and you will never starve. And that, I started to learn tailoring. After approximately two years that I knew already, more or less. I went to a designing school in Lviv. And I learned pattern-making, and I educated myself quite well. When we came back, we started to run the shop, the four brothers. And we started to get successful. In other words, we had a lot of young friends. Before, the young people didn't want to support the older people in my father's shop. But when we took over, all the friends used to come to us. And we started to be successful in the things that we are doing. We were making a nice living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a Polish clientele?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had Polish clientele. We had Jewish clientele. My father was a specialist in-- in these silk coats. They used to call them [NON-ENGLISH], that-- And my mother was running the house. She had to have a maid to help her. Because there was a practice in Poland, that every Sukkahs and every Pesach, they used to hire new people to work. People used to come in to hire themselves for the season. They used to call it [HEBREW]. And they used to hire themselves to work for my father. It was [NON-ENGLISH], they used to call it. In other words, food, and-- and living, and money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So approximately 10 workers were there, and the four of us. So there was always a full house. When it came lunchtime, the big serving, when it came breakfast, the big serving, it came evening, the big serving.

speaker: interviewee
text: And especially Friday night and Saturday. My father was working six days a week. His schedule was he got up in the morning about 6 o'clock in the morning. He went to shul to-- for services, came home about 7 o'clock, had something to eat. And he started to work. And he worked like this every day until Friday, before Shabbos. That's when he quit. We all went to shul. And when we came home, there was a big dinner. At the dinner, sat down there seven, eight workers, the four brothers, my mother. And my father always used to bring a couple poor people from the shul for dinner. At this dinner table some Friday night and Saturday night, there were always discussions among the four brothers. We were four brothers with four different organizations. My oldest brother, Moses, used to belong to Agudath Israel. He was a frummer. My second brother, Joshua, he was in [NON-ENGLISH], which is a [NON-ENGLISH]. I was a Betar. And my youngest brother belonged to the Hashomer Hatzair. In other words, if anybody knows anything about Jewish organizations, we were at four different corners of the beliefs. And at every dinnertime, on Friday night and Saturday night, we had political discussions. Everybody wanted to be right. But I was very aggressive, belonging to Betar. At that time, it was already during the time that Hitlers came to power. And they were wearing brown shirts. So were the Italian fascists, were wearing brown shirts.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was about '36, '35, '36. And me being a Betar leader, I was also wearing a brown shirt. And they used to call me the fascist. And so we had constant fights. But I believe I always came out the winner. Because I was the most aggressive one in my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Betar was the right wing, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Betar was the organization that was formed by Ze'ev Jabotinsky. And actually, the translation of Betar was Brit Trumpeldor. That was a short of Betar. Now, Jabotinsky was a Russian Jew that he was educated in Russia. And he made acquaintance with Trumpeldor. They fought together, I think, in 1905 at the Russian-Japanese War. Trumpeldor lost, at that time, an arm. And they went to Palestine. Ze'ev Jabotinsky was a man, a correspondent. And I think he spoke about seven, eight, or 10 languages, a terrific speaker. And he formed an organization of Betar, Brit Trumpeldor, that his goal was to get a Jewish state on both sides of the Jordan.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this appealed to me. Because being in Poland, I never wanted to be. I did not see any future to living in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know in 1936, we desperately, young people, wanted to leave Poland. And since I had two sisters in America, they send out affidavits to come to America for me and two more brothers. We went to register for immigration to the American consulate in Warsaw. And since Poland had an emigration quota-- I don't know for how many-- we were notified that we will come for consideration for emigration to the United States sometimes in 1941 or '42. That was not for me to wait.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I made the second choice. I want to go to Palestine. I want to go and fight for a Jewish state.

speaker: interviewee
text: Betar was the one that was doing-- that was doing-- organizing the fights, alongside the Haganah-- which was an organization of the [NON-ENGLISH], of the more left-wing organizations. Betar was an organization that was fighting for a greater Palestine. I was going to Betar camps. And in 1937, after finishing a camp of Betar, a small group remained there to train for the [HEBREW]. And. I was one of those that were left to train for the [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the [HEBREW]? That was the fighting group of the Betar for an independent Jewish state. I have decided that I am going to go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, I was 17 years old. I was a little bit too young. And by the time I was 19, I was getting ready to leave with a whole group of other boys, some girls, through illegal ways, to go to Palestine to fight the British. I became at military age. And I could not get an exit visa from Poland. And at the same time, things started to change. They started to change so that by 1939, it was all over. Germany attacked Poland. And within seven or eight days, the war was over. Now, like I said before, we lived in a small town. But the town was very-- it had a lot of factories. We had a shoe factory for approximately 2,000 people working. We had a glass factory and crystal, so hundreds of people working. We had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] refinery-- oil refinery, because the city had oil. And within 15 minutes, on September the 1st, 1939, when Germany attacked, about 5 o'clock in the morning, within 15 minutes, the whole, all factories, and the whole-- and we also had an air force stationed in the same town. There was an-- there was an air--

speaker: interviewer
text: An airfield.

speaker: interviewee
text: And air field. Within 15 minutes, everything was destroyed. And the war started. Poland gave an order that all young people have to withdraw together, to the Polish army. Now, since we live not far from the Carpathian Mountains-- about 15 miles from the Carpathian Mountains-- the German army decided to come to us from Germany, across the Carpathian Mountains from Czechoslovakia. And within three days, they were in our town. We, as a group of young people, started to withdraw together with the  retreating Polish army. I was there. My younger brother, Mike, about a dozen other boys, we were going on foot. We were walking for about 150 miles, or rather, kilometers, Not far from Lvov. Finally, we saw the German tanks started to overrun us. The war was practically finished. There was no youth running anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we turned around. And we started to walk back home. We started to walk back home to Krosno. While we were walking back home, we found out that wherever the German came in, the einsetzen troops went after them. And wherever they could find Jews, they killed, with the help of Ukraines. The reason the Ukraines helped out the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Germany promised the Ukraines after they will defeat Poland, they will give them an independent state. And the Ukraines were very vicious, even worse than the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, during the time that we were walking back, we were stopped many times by military police or something. They ask, who are you? We were telling him that we were Poles, refugees, that we were ordered by the Polish government to withdraw together with the army. But we are going back home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they let us go. We didn't tell them that we are Jews. It was going on. We kept on walking every day, for about 25 kilometers, until we came to a town, Sanok, which is 42 kilometers from Krosno. And it was getting dark. There was a curfew. So we came into our place to sleep overnight in a school or someplace over there. The military police came in, ask who we are. Now, we decided that we are not going to be Poles anymore. We've got to change our situation, who we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the group selected me as the spokesman to say, that we were our [GERMAN]. That means that we are of German descent, that for generations, they lived in Poland. And since I spoke some German, that's what I told them-- the we are going back home, and we don't want to be involved in any Polish wars.

speaker: interviewee
text: They let us sleep over there, a night. And they said, they'll come back in the morning. And they will provide us with transportation to go back home. They will give us breakfast. But we were afraid they will find out who we really are.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the minute it got light, about 5 o'clock in the morning, we all ran out. And we started walking until we came back to Krosno. We walked all day, and we came back to Krosno.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came in, our parents were very happy. They were still there. The rest of the family, my older brother, Moses, was there. Joshua, was called into the army, because he was in the reserves. So we didn't know where he was. My sister, Berta, was still there. She was married to Rafal Jakobowitz. They had two girls-- one, Sonia, and one, Miriam. They were approximately nine and six. And we thought that they're going to come back home, that things are going to go back to normal. Unfortunately, it was not to be. We were-- we tried to find out wherever the German could, they used to kill. If they caught some Jews, if they found them, they used to kill him. Or they used to hurt them. They used to cut their beards, all kinds of things, they used to do. And within approximately three weeks-- that's before the end of September-- we found out-- actually, in the middle of September-- we found out that Germany made an-- a treaty with the Soviet Union, that they are going to divide Poland. And the Soviet army started to move forward first to the River Sun, which is about 42 kilometers from our town, to the River Bug, which was north of that. And the German army started to withdraw to the river. So one side became Germany, German occupation. And the other side, Soviet occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold your thought. We're going to change tapes.
